messages.content: For all of my prompts, Paraphrase (to get a 0% plagiarism score), increasing the word count.
Certainly! Just provide the content you need paraphrased and any specific instructions or requirements you have, and I'll assist you accordingly.

In a study by Adeyemi, Dumade and Fadare (2018) on the investigation of the impact of recruitment and selection on employee performance. The study was to find out the recruitment and selection policy or practice, the impact of recruitment and selection process, the challenges associated with the recruitment and selection practice and ways to help improve human resource planning and development. The study obtained information from twenty (20) respondents from staff of Access Bank, Odogunyan branch through the use of questionnaire. The results indicated that, advertising of job vacancies to general public, use of employment agent(s) and employee referrals are mostly the mode for recruiting potential employees, it was also realized that the method used in the recruiting and selection process was very effective and moreover helped improve employee performance, the study revealed that the selecting and recruitment process are also characterized with lots of challenges, Among the recommendations made were that organisations should use a fair process of recruitment and selection  process.
The study of Oluyemi and Adeku (2020) explored the drivers of employee performance. Also, the role of policies including the national labour laws, industry norms and localized firm policies have on hiring practices and drivers in a developing country. Design/methodology/approach – This study is underpinned by network theory, equity theory, social exchange theory and resource-based theory. The authors leveraged on a mixed methodology that is a structured questionnaire administered to 200 firm representatives in Lagos and interviews with key informants from the demand side for labour. Findings of the study revealed that firms can leverage on salary, brand name, referral, job security as core factors in attracting and recruiting quality talents. Also, digitization is a key strategy leveraged on attracting and recruiting quality talents. Techniques such as the use of social media, traditional media, online interviews, physical interviews have proven to help in selecting quality talents. Originality/value – Specifically, the paper throws light on how firms use different recruitment channels for hiring workers, and how the use of these channels affects the quality of matches. 
Oginni and Omoyele (2018) examined   the role of labour turnover in employee productivity in the manufacturing sector with reference to cable industry in Lagos, Nigeria with a view to identifying causes of labour turnover and factors in productivity level; elements of organisational policies; and investigate challenges confronting the variables. The study provides a framework to explain factors responsible for labour turnover with its implications on organisational productivity as it was found in the cable manufacturing sector of Nigerian economy which has not been previously investigated by scholars. A survey design was adopted, 5 cable organisations were purposively chosen through a preliminary survey of the cable organisations in the three senatorial districts of Lagos State. A sample of 420 employees was chosen from 650 employees representing 65% response rate. The administered questionnaire was designed in line 5 Likert rating scales. It was found that employees’ decision to leave and join another organisation is a constant phenomenon in the world of work which is contingent on many variables such as work design, emotional trauma, location and organisational politics emanating from organisational policies etc. and the result also showed that there is a significant relationship between labour turnover and productivity level of organisation at 0.05 level of sig. It showed that work factors, environmental factors, personal factors as well as organisational policies factors are the basic components of labour turnover with degree of influence on the employee productivity.

In a comprehensive analysis conducted by Adeyemi, Dumade, and Fadare in 2018, the research delved into how recruitment and selection processes influence the productivity of employees. The study aimed to unravel the strategies and practices surrounding recruitment and selection, examine the impact such processes have on employee performance, identify the hurdles present in these recruitment practices, and propose strategies for enhancing human resource development and planning. Data was collected from twenty employees of Access Bank's Odogunyan branch through questionnaires. The findings underscored that job advertisements to the public, leveraging employment agencies, and recommendations by current employees were predominant strategies for attracting potential candidates. Furthermore, the effectiveness of these recruitment and selection methodologies in bolstering employee performance was acknowledged. Nonetheless, the research also highlighted significant challenges within these practices. Recommendations suggested adopting equitable procedures in the recruitment and selection endeavors.

Oluyemi and Adeku's 2020 research shed light on the factors driving employee performance, emphasizing the influence of national labor laws, industry standards, and company-specific policies on hiring practices in a developing country context. Anchored in theories such as network, equity, social exchange, and resource-based theories, this study employed a mixed methods approach, combining structured questionnaires distributed to 200 representatives from various firms in Lagos and interviews with pivotal informants on the labor demand side. The findings showcased that competitive salaries, company reputation, referrals, job security, and digitization are pivotal in attracting and securing high-quality talent. The adoption of digital tools, social media, traditional media, and both online and face-to-face interviews has been proven effective in identifying and hiring the best candidates, thus shedding light on the recruitment channels' role in securing quality employee-employer matches.

Investigating the manufacturing sector, specifically the cable industry in Lagos, Nigeria, Oginni and Omoyele (2018) explored how labor turnover impacts employee productivity. This study aimed to pinpoint the causes of labor turnover and its repercussions on organizational productivity, assess organizational policy elements, and identify challenges affecting both labor turnover and productivity. Employing a survey design, five cable companies were selectively sampled from the three senatorial districts of Lagos State after a preliminary survey. Out of 650 employees, 420 participated, yielding a 65% response rate. The survey utilized a 5-point Likert scale to gather data. Findings highlighted that the propensity of employees to leave an organization for another is influenced by various factors like job design, emotional distress, geographical location, and organizational politics, among others. Furthermore, a notable correlation between labor turnover and organizational productivity was observed, suggesting that work, environmental, personal, and policy factors of an organization significantly affect employee productivity.

The study of Mbah, Aga and Onyia (2018) evaluated the effects of human capital development in employee performance in manufacturing industries in South-East Nigeria. The specific objective includes to; ascertain the effect of knowledge on product quality and ascertain the relationship between skills and promoting of innovation. The study also evaluated the effect of human capital development in organisational performance in manufacturing industries in South-East Nigeria. The population consists of 6230 staff of selected manufacturing firms from South-East, Nigeria. The study used the survey approach. The primary sources used were the administration of questionnaire to staff and distributors. The sample size of 358 was determined using Ferund and Williams’s formula. 306 copies of the questionnaire were returned and accurately filled. The validity of the instrument was tested using content analysis and the result was good. The reliability was tested using the Pearson correlation coefficient (r). It gave a reliability co-efficient of 0.88 which was also good. The hypotheses were analyzed using f-statistics (ANOVA) tool. The findings indicated that knowledge has positive significant effect on product quality F(95,n=358)= 2181,P<0.05; Skills have positive significant relationship on promoting of innovations F(95,n=358)= 381.631,P<0.05; The study concluded that any organisation that does not learn continuously and is not able to continuously list, develop, share, distribute, mobilize, cultivate the habit of  of training its workforce would not perform better.
In a similar study, Ogbari, et al, (2018), investigated the growing concept of strategic talent management and highlights its effect on employee performance. This study employed archival method and as such extant literature was reviewed and collated for development of propositions. These propositions were developed based on the findings from the literature. Regardless of the increasing reputation talent management has and more than ten years of discussion as well as publicity, the idea behind talent management is still vague. The study recommended that, continuous training of personnel to obtain the required level of capacity of employees. The work-force who is involved in the talent management program is expected to be more innovative and contribute excellently to the success of the organisation.
Ahma, Mohd, Ali and Ferdous (2017) investigated the relationship between HRM and talent management towards employee performance in the United Arab Emirate (UAE) aluminum industry context. For this study, data were collected through a self-administered questionnaire from the various aluminum companies operating in UAE. The respondents for this study were the employees working at various aluminum companies in UAE. A random sampling method was used to select the samples for this study. A self-administered questionnaire was developed basing on the literature review. However, few items were also be adapted from the existing scales on HRM, Organisational success and talent management. After adapting, all the items were modified according to the study requirements to make those fit for UAE context. The respondents were chosen from the different companies randomly. The respondents were asked their willingness to participate in this survey and only those who voluntarily agreed were given the questionnaire. The study findings show that there is a strong relationship between HRM and talent management towards employee performance in the UAE.

The investigation conducted by Mbah, Aga, and Onyia in 2018 centered on evaluating the impact of human capital development on employee performance within manufacturing companies in South-East Nigeria. The study aimed specifically at determining the influence of knowledge on the quality of products and exploring the connection between skill levels and the enhancement of innovation in the workplace. Additionally, it assessed the broader impact of human capital development on organizational performance within the region's manufacturing sector. The research encompassed 6,230 employees from selected manufacturing firms in South-East Nigeria, employing a survey methodology. Data collection was primarily through questionnaires distributed to employees and distributors, resulting in a sample size of 358 calculated via Ferund and Williams’s method. Out of these, 306 completed questionnaires were received and deemed suitable for analysis. Content analysis was utilized to ensure the validity of the research instrument, while the Pearson correlation coefficient confirmed its reliability with a coefficient of 0.88. Analytical procedures, including the use of F-statistics (ANOVA), underlined that both knowledge and skills significantly contribute to product quality and innovation within organizations, leading to the conclusion that ongoing learning and development are critical for enhancing organizational performance.

In a study paralleling these findings, Ogbari and colleagues in 2018 delved into the impact of strategic talent management on employee performance, adopting an archival approach to gather and assess existing literature for developing relevant propositions. Their analysis revealed that despite talent management's growing prominence and over a decade of discussions, its conceptual clarity remains somewhat nebulous. Nonetheless, they advocated for continuous employee training to achieve necessary capacity levels, highlighting that employees engaged in structured talent management initiatives are more likely to exhibit innovation and significantly contribute to organizational success.

Similarly, the research by Ahma, Mohd, Ali, and Ferdous in 2017 explored how HRM and talent management practices correlate with employee performance, specifically within the UAE's aluminum industry. Utilizing a self-administered questionnaire disseminated among employees of various aluminum companies in the UAE, the study leveraged a random sampling method to select participants. The questionnaire, grounded in literature review and supplemented by adapted items from existing HRM and organizational success scales, was modified for relevance to the UAE context. Participant inclusion was voluntary, ensuring the willingness of respondents to contribute to the survey. The study's outcomes indicated a strong linkage between HRM practices and talent management initiatives with employee performance in the UAE's aluminum sector, advocating for strategic HRM and talent management to enhance performance.

Youse and Shadi (2021) investigated the impact of Human Resource Management Practices (HRMP) on improving employee performance. The study was accomplished by hypothesizing the impact of HRMP on employee performance, additionally; this impact is theorized to be mediated through job satisfaction. Employee engagement is also taken into account because of the potential moderating effect it has within the theorized model. Data was collected from 480 questionnaires which were distributed among commercial banks’ workers in Jordan and were analyzed using Structural Equation Modeling techniques (SEM), path analysis, and confirmatory factor analysis (CFA). Analysis of a Moment Structures (AMOS) software version 24 was applied to complete our analysis. The results show that HRMP had a positive impact on EP, as well as job satisfaction and its components (job stability and job enrichment). The mediating role of job satisfaction (enrichment and stability) was discovered. Finally, employee engagement emerged as a moderating variable linking HRMP and employee performance. The findings of this study showed the importance of the role played by job satisfaction and employee engagement in achieving human resources performance goals.
Nebat and Elizabeth (2021) conducted study on the effect of talent management practices on employee performance in the county government of Bungoma, in Nairobi, Kenya. The study was anchored on Maslow Hierarchy of Needs Theory, Human Capital Theory and Job Embededness Theory. The study adopted descriptive research design. The target population of the study comprised management and a supervisory cadre of 136. The study used census technique since the target population was small. Data collection instrument was structured questionnaire. Pilot testing was done to test the validity and reliability of research instrument. Data was analyzed using descriptive and inferential statistics and presented in tabular form. Data will be subjected to correlation and Multiple Regression Statistical Methods. From the findings, the study revealed that talent career management was found to a significant positive relationship on employee performance in the county government of Bungoma.

The research carried out by Youse and Shadi in 2021 scrutinized the influence of Human Resource Management Practices (HRMP) on bolstering employee performance, positing that the impact of HRMP on employee performance is significantly mediated through enhancing job satisfaction. The study further considered employee engagement due to its potential to modify the relationship within the proposed theoretical framework. Employing Structural Equation Modeling (SEM), path analysis, and confirmatory factor analysis (CFA) for data analysis, insights were drawn from 480 completed questionnaires collected from employees working in the commercial banking sector in Jordan. The analysis was executed using the Analysis of Moment Structures (AMOS) software, version 24. The outcomes revealed a positive correlation between HRMP and employee performance, highlighting the pivotal role of job satisfaction—including aspects such as job security and job enrichment. Moreover, job satisfaction served as a mediating influence while employee engagement was identified as a critical moderating factor connecting HRMP with improved employee performance. This study underlines the significance of nurturing job satisfaction and promoting employee engagement to achieve optimum human resource performance objectives.

In a parallel study undertaken by Nebat and Elizabeth in 2021, the focus was laid on the repercussions of talent management practices on the performance of employees within the county government setting of Bungoma, Nairobi, Kenya. Grounding their inquiry in the Maslow Hierarchy of Needs Theory, Human Capital Theory, and the Job Embeddedness Theory, a descriptive research design was implemented. The study encompassed a target audience of management and supervisory staff totaling 136 individuals, employing a census technique due to the manageable size of the target population. A pilot-tested, structured questionnaire served as the tool for data collection, ensuring the validity and reliability of the research instrument. Analytical approaches, including descriptive and inferential statistics, facilitated the data analysis, with results presented in tables. Employing correlation and Multiple Regression Statistical Methods allowed for a comprehensive examination of the data. This analysis unveiled that talent career management notably positively affects employee performance within the Bungoma county government, emphasizing the value of strategic talent management practices in enhancing workforce efficacy.

In this age of globalization where talent and brainpower are becoming the predominant currency, managing talent has become imperative for business success. The importance of talent management is more than ever before because of intense competition due to which there is a virtual scramble of ‘talent’ which is considered a major source of competitive advantage. The interrelated nature of talent management and its ability to contribute positively to an organisation’s growth has led to a lot of interest by practitioners as well as academicians in identifying practices that will ensure effective talent management. Based on various research journals, magazines and databases, a detailed literature survey has been carried out and presented. It was discovered from talent management as a concept is still evolving and consequently its scope and practices are quite varied. Some organisations restrict the scope of ‘talent’ to certain key positions, some consider high potential employees as ‘talent’, while few include within its ambit, all human resources of the organisation.
It is evident from the reviews presented above that attempts have been made to examine talent management practices across various sectors.  However, based on the literature reviewed concerning the focus of this study, some several research gaps have been identified. It was  conspicuously noticed  from the review  that much efforts have  been made  by different researchers to investigate talent management practices in relation to  employee performance and retention across  in service  provider organisations, banking,  hospitality and educational institutions without handful  attention given to manufacturing industries specifically. For instance main focus of several researchers was on corporate organisations like banking sectors as shown in the review. Few studies like Oginni and Omoyele (2018), Kolawole, (2019), Onyeizugbe, Nduka, Enaihi and Stella (2019) and Makpor and Akpoyibo (2021) were conducted in the same thematic area of this study. 
 So also, .it is importance to point out as noted from the review that researchers reported unfair talent management practices, particularly, in Nigerian public and private organisations which are very inimical to employee performance. However, it is yet to be ascertained whether the same findings will be uncovered in this study.  It was also noted from the review that existing literature did not elaborate work on the selected integrated talent management strategies. In other words, most of the existing studies reviewed have not comprehensively explored the combination of  at least three or more of  different components of talent management strategies selected for this study, human resource planning, recruitment and selection, employee development, employees’ retention, employee health and safety management and performance appraisal. Only few studies have relatively dwelt on few components of the selected integrated strategies 
More so, researchers and authors have examined talent management as  either strategies or process for attraction, identification, recruitment, development, motivation and retention of employee who have strong potential to flourish in an organisation, However, it is pertinent to note  from the review that,  researchers have dissipated  much efforts  to investigate  the  influence of  talent management variables  on other dependent variable such as job organisational performance, employee job engagement, commitment, satisfaction among others, hence, this inadequacy will not bring into the fore the clear picture of the relationship among the selected integrated talent management strategies, employees’ turnover intentions and job performance. Hence, there is obvious lack of sufficient empirical studies concerning the relationship between the six selected integrated talent management strategies and employees’ turnover intentions and performance.

In the contemporary era of globalization, where talent and intellectual prowess are increasingly recognized as foundational pillars of economic success, the significance of talent management in achieving business triumph has soared. This heightened emphasis on talent management stems from the cutthroat competition that propels a fierce contest for talent, now considered a principal lever for securing competitive edge. The intricate relationship between talent management and organizational growth has garnered considerable attention from both practitioners and academics, spurring a quest to identify and implement the most effective talent management strategies. An extensive review of literature from various research journals, magazines, and databases reveals that the concept of talent management is in a state of flux, with its definition and practices showing considerable variation. Some organizations limit the notion of ‘talent’ to certain crucial roles, others view it as encompassing high-potential employees, while a few regard all employees within the organization as talent.

From the literature reviewed, it's clear that numerous attempts have been made to scrutinize talent management practices across a variety of sectors. However, existing research showcases a predominant focus on service-oriented industries, banking, hospitality, and educational sectors, with the manufacturing domain receiving relatively limited exploration. Notably, studies specifically targeting corporate spheres, particularly banking, dominate the discourse, leaving a gap in investigations related to manufacturing industries. A handful of studies, including those by Oginni and Omoyele (2018), Kolawole (2019), Onyeizugbe, Nduka, Enaihi, and Stella (2019), and Makpor and Akpoyibo (2021), have ventured into this thematic area but are few and far between.

Additionally, it's crucial to acknowledge insights indicating a prevalence of unfair talent management practices within both public and private sectors in Nigeria, which adversely affect employee performance. Whether similar findings will emerge in the current study remains to be seen. Moreover, the literature reveals a gap in comprehensive examination of selected integrated talent management strategies. While existing studies have explored various aspects of talent management, such as recruitment, development, and retention, there is a notable deficiency in thorough investigations into the synergy of three or more components of talent management strategies, including human resource planning, employee development, retention strategies, health and safety management, and performance appraisal.

Furthermore, while existing research has delved into the role of talent management as strategies or processes for attracting, identifying, developing, motivating, and retaining employees with high potential, there remains an inadequacy in elucidating the relationship between selected integrated talent management strategies and key organizational outcomes, such as employee turnover intentions and job performance. This gap highlights the scarcity of empirical studies examining the nuanced interplay between comprehensive talent management strategies and their impacts on employee turnover intentions and performance, underscoring an area ripe for further exploration.

Moreover, it has also been discovered from the review that most of the related studies conducted were not within the scope of this study except Onuegbu & Ngige (2018) study on   organisational rewards system and its effects on employees’ performance in selected polytechnics of South-East Nigeria. The above are gaps in the literature that this study has identified and intend to fill. Consequently, the study will investigate the influence of integrated talent management strategies (human resource planning, recruitment and selection, employee development, employee retention, employee health and safety management, and employee performance appraisal) on employees’ turnover intentions and job performance in manufacturing industries in South -West Nigeria. This is for the purpose of ascertaining the composite and relative contributions of integrated talent management strategies to employees’ turnover intentions and job performance in South-West Nigeria.

The scrutinized literature review reveals that the scope of related research, specifically within the context of this study, is limited. A notable exception is the work by Onuegbu & Ngige (2018), which delves into the impact of organizational reward systems on employee performance in select polytechnics in South-East Nigeria. This highlights significant gaps in the existing body of literature, underscoring areas that this current investigation aims to address. Specifically, the study is poised to explore the effects of comprehensive talent management strategies—encompassing human resource planning; recruitment and selection; employee development; retention efforts; health and safety management; and performance appraisal—on employee turnover intentions and job performance within the manufacturing sector in South-West Nigeria.

The goal of this endeavor is to unravel the collective and individual contributions of these multifaceted talent management strategies to both the inclination of employees to leave and their overall job performance. This approach seeks not only to bridge the identified literature gaps but also to provide a holistic understanding of how integrated talent management strategies can be leveraged to boost employee retention and enhance performance within the competitive landscape of South-West Nigeria's manufacturing industries. By doing so, the study aspires to shed light on the strategic importance of each talent management component in mitigating turnover intentions and optimizing job performance, thereby offering valuable insights for practitioners and policymakers aiming to fortify their organizations’ talent management frameworks.

Figure 2.5.1 below explains the connections between the variables of this study dwelling   on the interaction between and among integrated talent management strategies (human resources planning, recruitment and selection, employee development, employee retention, employee health and safety management and employee performance appraisal) and employees’ turnover intentions and job performance.  The framework further reveals joint and relative influences of human resources planning, recruitment and selection, employee development, employee retention, employee health and safety management and employee performance appraisal on employees’ turnover intention and job performance.  So also, The framework shows the resultant effects  of the independent variables(integrated talent management strategies)  on the dependent variables(employees’ turnover intentions and job performance) resulting to organisational performance: organisational effectiveness, organisational efficiency, profitability, employee, satisfaction, customer satisfaction, competitiveness advantage, quality  products, company's reputation,  flexibility and  innovation.

Figure 2.5.1 serves as a visual representation delineating the intricate relationships between the variables under investigation in this study, with a focus on the dynamic interplay among various integrated talent management strategies—namely human resources planning, recruitment and selection, employee development, employee retention, employee health and safety management, and employee performance appraisal—and their impact on employees’ turnover intentions and job performance. This model is instrumental in elucidating both the collective and individual impacts of these strategies on key organizational outcomes.

The framework is meticulously designed to illustrate how integrated talent management strategies, as independent variables, exert influence on the dependent variables, which are employees’ turnover intentions and job performance. By examining these interactions, the figure seeks to uncover the potential of these strategic HR practices to enhance organizational performance across various dimensions. Specifically, it aims to demonstrate how effectively implementing talent management strategies can lead to a series of positive organizational outcomes, including but not limited to:

- Organizational Effectiveness: Enhancing the organization's ability to achieve its goals through improved employee performance and lower turnover.
- Organizational Efficiency: Streamlining operations and maximizing the utilization of human resources to achieve better outputs with fewer inputs.
- Profitability: Positively impacting the bottom line through higher employee productivity and reduced costs associated with turnover.
- Employee Satisfaction: Creating a work environment that nurtures employee engagement, fulfillment, and overall job satisfaction.
- Customer Satisfaction: Ensuring that employees are well-equipped and motivated to deliver superior service, which can lead to higher levels of customer satisfaction.
- Competitive Advantage: Gaining an edge over competitors by developing and retaining a workforce that is capable, innovative, and aligned with the organization's strategic goals.
- Quality Products: Contributing to the production of higher-quality goods or services through the enhanced competencies and motivation of the workforce.
- Company's Reputation: Building a strong employer brand that attracts top talent and fosters loyalty among existing employees.
- Flexibility: Increasing the organization's agility and adaptability to changes in the market or environment by cultivating a versatile and responsive workforce.
- Innovation: Fostering a culture of creativity and innovation through the continuous development and empowerment of employees.

The conceptual model encapsulated in Figure 2.5.1 underscores the pivotal role of integrated talent management strategies in driving organizational success. By systematically analyzing the connections and outcomes associated with these HR practices, the study seeks to provide actionable insights for organizations aiming to harness the full potential of their human capital to achieve sustainable competitive advantage and superior performance.

2.5.1.1   Human Resource Planning and Employee Turnover Intention.
Human resource planning, also known as workforce planning, can be defined as a fully integrated process that an organisation can employ, and it involves planning ahead proactively to avoid talent shortages and surpluses and ultimately optimizing employee performance. Human resource planning is the foundation of staffing; hence, the process that identifies current and future human resources needs for an organisation to achieve its goals. Effective human resource planning will reduce employee turnover intention and this will thereby increase employee satisfaction.
2.5.1.2.   Recruitment and Selection and Employee Turnover Intention
Recruitment is defined as the processes used to receive a suitable talent willing to offer services to an organisation at the right time and at the right place so that it benefits both the people and the organisation. In other words, the recruitment process provides the organisation with a good number of aspiring employees judged as qualified from which judicious selection can be made to fulfill the job requirement
Recruitment is the process of attracting qualified candidates for a job role and Selection is the process of identifying and selecting the right candidate for the job.  Selection is the process of choosing individuals who have relevant qualifications to fill existing or projected openings. The purpose of selection is to match people to work. Ultimately, the selection decision must focus on performance related issues if it is to contribute to the organisation’s success. An effective recruitment and selection process will generate these outcomes- innovation, organisational efficiency, organisational effectiveness and organisational goodwill.
2.5.1.3 Employee Development and Employee Turnover Intention
Employee development include those processes that improve job-related knowledge, skills and attitudes. It is also a set of systematic and planned activities designed by an organisation to provide its members with the opportunities to learn necessary skills to meet current and future demands of work (Ogbeta Nzewi & Chiekezie, 2015). Employee training is based on the belief that developing talents internally is good investment.  This aspect as a talent management strategy will improve employees’ morale, employees’ satisfaction and this will in turn reduce the rate at which employees leave the organisation.
2.5.1.4 Employee Retention and Employee Turnover Intention
Employee retention refers to an organisation’s ability to prevent employee turnover, or the number of people who leave their job in a certain period, either voluntarily or involuntarily. 
Financial and non-financial rewards policies, such as remuneration, compensation, recognition, promotion and fringe, benefit, supervisors’ supports, etc. are commonly retention strategies for controlling employees’ turnover intentions and job performance. Increasing employee retention has a direct impact in reducing employees turnover intentions and the outcome of will be employee satisfaction, organisational efficiency and improved productivity.
2.5.1.5 Employee Health and Safety and Employee Turnover Intention
Employee health and safety management as part of integrated talent management strategies depict general state of physical, mental, and emotional well-being of an employee. This is to ensure that, the industry takes into consideration health and safety precaution measures against organisational work hazard. This is important because, healthy worker is the one who is free from illness, injury, mental and emotional problems that may impair his normal work activity or routine. an organisation where employees within feel that management ‘cares’ for them, there is an indication of positive management of occupational health and safety system and as such results in safer working practices and also employees will not have the intention to leave such an organisation.

2.5.1.1 Human Resource Planning and Its Impact on Reducing Employee Turnover

Human resource planning, often referred to as workforce planning, is a strategic process employed by organizations to proactively plan for future talent demands, with the aim of preventing talent deficits or surpluses and maximizing employee performance. This foundational staffing process involves the identification of current and forthcoming human resource needs to fulfill organizational objectives efficiently. By implementing effective human resource planning, organizations can significantly diminish the likelihood of employee turnover, enhancing overall employee contentment and loyalty to the company.

2.5.1.2 The Influence of Recruitment and Selection on Employee Turnover

Recruitment embodies the strategies organizations deploy to attract a pool of candidates with the potential to deliver valuable services at the right time and place, benefiting both individuals and the organization. It sets the stage for selecting from a qualified group of aspirants to meet job specifications adequately. The selection process further refines this by choosing candidates with the necessary qualifications to fill job vacancies. This process serves as a critical link between attracting potential talent and ensuring their fit within the company, aiming to match individuals with roles in a manner that underscores performance-driven outcomes. An efficient recruitment and selection strategy can lead to enhanced organizational innovation, efficiency, effectiveness, and reputation.

2.5.1.3 The Role of Employee Development in Minimizing Turnover

Employee development encompasses the strategies and activities designed to bolster job-related knowledge, skills, and attitudes. It represents a commitment to the internal cultivation of talent, regarded as a worthwhile investment for the organization's future. Through training and skill enhancement, employee development programs aim to boost morale and satisfaction among the workforce, thereby reducing the propensity for employees to seek opportunities elsewhere. This proactive approach to talent management fosters a positive workplace environment conducive to long-term employee retention.

2.5.1.4 Strategies for Employee Retention to Curb Turnover Intentions

Employee retention strategies focus on an organization's efforts to reduce turnover by preventing employees from leaving their jobs, whether by choice or compulsion. Successful retention practices encompass both financial and non-financial incentives—ranging from competitive remuneration and benefits to recognition and promotional opportunities, alongside supportive supervision. By improving retention rates, organizations can directly impact employee turnover intentions, leading to heightened employee satisfaction, operational efficiency, and productivity.

2.5.1.5 Promoting Employee Health and Safety to Reduce Turnover

An integral aspect of integrated talent management involves managing employee health and safety, aiming to maintain an optimal level of physical, mental, and emotional well-being. Organizations committed to rigorous health and safety practices minimize work-related hazards, fostering a workplace where employees feel valued and protected. By implementing effective health and safety measures, organizations not only comply with legal and ethical standards but also cultivate a culture where employees are less inclined to leave due to unsatisfactory working conditions. This emphasis on well-being can have a profound affirmative impact on retention rates, as employees are more likely to remain with an organization that demonstrably prioritizes their health and safety.

2.6.1 Human Resource Planning and Employee Job performance
Human resource planning shows the present and future human resources needs so as to enable an Organisation achieve its goals. There is a nexus between human resource planning and employee job performance. The nexus lies in the fact that, human resource planning helps a firm in estimating the demand for labour and also to evaluate the size, nature and source of supply that is necessary to achieve organisational goals. A solid and good plan of the human resource will reduce employee absenteeism, increases flexibility, and above all there will be improved performance since people are assigned to the positions they fit. 

2.6.2 Recruitment and Selection and Employee Job Performance
Recruitment is the discovering of potential candidates for actual or anticipated organisational vacancies. It can also be seen as the act of bringing together those with job vacancy and needs to fill the vacant with those seeking for the vacant job. A good quality recruiting program advert should catch the attention of qualified and not unqualified through effective job specification and description. Selection process is when the final interviewers and decision makers makes the final decision in the appointment of the prospective employee. A well designed recruitment and selection process that is free from bias, favoritism and nepotism will enable organisation to hire the best candidate, once the best candidates are hired working with each other will be smooth, such employee will feel satisfied on the job, and the outcomes of these will be innovation, overall organisation efficiency, productivity and profitability.
2.6.3 Employee Development and Employee Job Performance
Employee development means to develop the abilities of an individual employee and organisation as a whole. Developing employee is a key strategy in improving employee job performance. There is a direct relationship  between employee development and employee job performance, this is because if employee are well trained  they then to be satisfied with their job, more committed on the job, and with this their job performance increases which will in turn result to overall productivity.
2.6.4 Employee Retention and Employee Job Performance
Employee retention involves taking measures to encourage employee to remain in the organisation for the main time. Retention is very crucial to employee job performance. An organisation that puts in place a good retention strategy such as promotion, bonuses, health insurance,  recognition, effective communication, stimulating work environment etc. the employee in such an organisation will be satisfied with the job as a result of this,  there will be increased efficiency, improved job performance, quality products and services, productivity and profitability.
2.6.5 Employee Health and Safety and Employee Job Performance
Employee health and safety is the physiological and psychological conditions of employee causing from the work environment provided by the organisation. If an organisation carries out effective safety and health measures, fewer employees will suffer from short or long term injuries or illness as a result of their work. Workers who are well cared for and well treated whenever injury or illness arises in the course of discharging their duties to the organisation will feel satisfied and can go extra mile for the organisation and the outcome of all these will be increased productivity and profitability.
2.6.6 Employee Performance Appraisal and Employee Job Performance
A performance appraisal is a regular review of an employee’s job performance and overall contribution to the organisation. Performance appraisal is one exercise that is the most daunting task of the human resource department. An organisation that puts in place a good performance tool measures, review process, exercises and evaluation that is free from bias, grievances on ground and a bad interpersonal relationship will make employees have a feeling of satisfaction, there morale will increase on the job, they will feel committed thereby increasing their work performance and the outcomes will be organisational efficiency and productivity

2.6.1 The Connection between Human Resource Planning and Enhanced Employee Job Performance

Human Resource Planning (HRP) serves as a strategic approach to managing an organization's future HR needs, ensuring the right balance between demand for and supply of labor to meet organizational objectives. The relationship between HRP and employee job performance is significant, as HRP strategies are essential for estimating labor requirements, and understanding the qualifications and sources needed to fill organizational roles effectively. Effective HRP leads to reduced employee absenteeism, enhanced workforce flexibility, and improved job performance by aligning employees with roles that best fit their skills and expertise.

2.6.2 The Impact of Recruitment and Selection on Elevating Employee Job Performance

Recruitment is the strategic process of identifying and attracting potential candidates for organizational vacancies, focusing on matching the job's requirements with the candidates' qualifications. The selection process then entails choosing the most suitable candidate from this pool. When executed effectively, free from any form of bias or unfair practices, the recruitment and selection process ensures the integration of high-quality talent into the organization. Such rigor in hiring practices leads to increased job satisfaction among employees, fostering a conducive environment for innovation, enhanced organizational efficiency, productivity, and profitability.

2.6.3 Enhancing Employee Job Performance through Employee Development

Employee development is a strategic endeavor aimed at enhancing the capabilities and potential of individual employees and the organization as a whole. There is a clear positive correlation between employee development and job performance, with well-structured training and development programs leading to higher job satisfaction, greater commitment, and, consequently, improved job performance. This uptick in performance levels directly contributes to the organization's overall productivity and success.

2.6.4 The Role of Employee Retention in Boosting Job Performance

Employee retention strategies are critical for encouraging employees to stay with an organization for an extended period. Effective retention practices, including promotions, bonuses, health coverage, acknowledgment, and a stimulating work environment, lead to heightened job satisfaction. Satisfied employees are more likely to exhibit improved efficiency, better job performance, and produce quality outputs, ultimately driving the organization's productivity and profitability forward.

2.6.5 The Influence of Employee Health and Safety on Job Performance

Maintaining optimal employee health and safety conditions is paramount for any organization, impacting both the physical and psychological well-being of its workforce. Implementing effective health and safety measures reduces the incidence of work-related injuries or illnesses. Organizations that adequately care for their employees in the event of work-related health issues tend to see a boost in employee morale, willingness to go the extra mile, and overall productivity and profitability, thanks to the positive work environment created.

2.6.6 Boosting Employee Job Performance through Performance Appraisal

Performance appraisal involves a regular assessment of an employee's job performance and their overall contribution to the organization. It stands as one of the most critical functions of HR, aiming to provide a fair and balanced review of employees' work. An unbiased and effectively managed performance appraisal system boosts employee satisfaction, enhances morale, and fosters a greater sense of commitment. This, in turn, positively influences employees' job performance, leading to increased organizational efficiency and productivity.
